steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has an independent clause followed by 'though' used postpositively (after the main clause), then a noun phrase — not another independent clause — explaining the limiting reason. Postpositive 'though' must be set off with commas on both sides: IC, though, [noun phrase].

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — electricity; though,: semicolons connect two independent clauses. The portion after 'though' is a noun phrase, not an independent clause, so a semicolon here is grammatically wrong. ✗
  • Choice B — electricity, though;: same problem — a semicolon after 'though' would require an independent clause to follow, but a noun phrase follows instead. ✗
  • Choice C — electricity, though: omitting the trailing comma makes 'though' read as a subordinating conjunction directly attached to the noun phrase, which breaks the parenthetical framing the sentence requires. ✗
  • Choice D — electricity, though,: correctly treats 'though' as a parenthetical transition word set off on both sides, producing the structure IC, though, [noun phrase]. ✓
That matches Choice D — electricity, though,.
